blocks.statement: I can write KE = ½mv², compute KE for moving objects across mass and speed scales, and state the work-energy theorem W_net = ΔKE.

blocks.whatWeKnow: The DAMAGE number for asteroids is their KINETIC ENERGY at impact, not their momentum.
blocks.connection: Today's equation is the heart of Unit 4. By Day 19, we plug 2026-XJ into it and get its yield in tons of TNT.

blocks.markdown:
## The squared-v reveal

Day 1 you guessed it. Here it is:

**KE = ½ · m · v²** — the energy of motion. Units: Joules (kg·m²/s²). Always positive, regardless of direction.

The load-bearing feature is the **squared v**. KE is NOT proportional to v — it's proportional to v². Doubling speed **quadruples** KE. Tripling speed gives **nine times** the KE.

And here's the link back to yesterday: **W_net = ΔKE = KE_f − KE_i**. The NET work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy. Push energy in (positive net work) → it speeds up. Drag energy out (negative net work) → it slows down. This is the **work-energy theorem**, and it's the engine of the whole unit.

terms.definition: The energy of motion. KE = ½ · m · v². Units: Joules (kg·m²/s²). Always positive, regardless of direction. A 1500-kg car at 30 m/s has KE = ½·1500·900 = 675,000 J. At 60 m/s: KE = 2,700,000 J — FOUR times as much.
terms.cognate: Sp. energía cinética · Pt. energia cinética · HC enèji sinetik

terms.definition: The NET work done on an object equals the change in its KE: W_net = ΔKE = KE_f − KE_i. A 1-kg cart at rest gets 50 J of net work → KE_f = 50 J → v_f = √(2·50/1) = 10 m/s. It's the NET work that matters — the sum of ALL forces' contributions, not just one.
terms.cognate: Sp. teorema del trabajo-energía · Pt. teorema do trabalho-energia

blocks.title: The squared v matters
blocks.markdown: KE is NOT proportional to v — it's proportional to v². Doubling speed quadruples KE.

blocks.prompt: A 1500-kg car moves at (a) 30 m/s, (b) 60 m/s. Find the KE in each case.
blocks.answer: At 30 m/s: 6.75 × 10⁵ J. At 60 m/s: 2.7 × 10⁶ J — FOUR times the energy.

blocks.title: Why highway speeds are different
blocks.markdown: Highway speeds aren't twice as dangerous as 30 mph. They're FOUR TIMES. This is the squared v at work — and it's why 2026-XJ matters.

blocks.prompt: KE OF 2026-XJ — 2026-XJ has m = 1 × 10⁹ kg and impact speed v = 3 × 10⁴ m/s. Find its KE at impact.
blocks.givenHint: m = 1 × 10⁹ kg · v = 3 × 10⁴ m/s. Square the speed FIRST, then multiply.
blocks.equationHint: Same equation as the car — just bigger numbers. Keep the powers of 10 organized.

blocks.prompt: WORK-ENERGY THEOREM — A 2-kg ball moves at 3 m/s. A net 27 J of work is done on it. What's its final speed? (Use W_net = ΔKE.)
blocks.givenHint: m = 2 kg · v_i = 3 m/s → KE_i = 9 J · W_net = 27 J → KE_f = ?
blocks.equationHint: W_net = KE_f − KE_i. Find KE_f first, then solve ½mv_f² = KE_f for v_f.

blocks.prompt: A 2-kg ball at 3 m/s has 9 J of KE. If a net 27 J of work is done on it, what's its final speed? Then complete the frame.
blocks.frame: Doubling a car's speed ___ the kinetic energy, because KE depends on v ___ (not just v).

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **9.3** and **9.5–9.6** (mechanical energy, kinetic energy, the work-energy theorem). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
